turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Gorsuch has our opinion in case 16-969, SAS Institute v. Iancu.

turns.name: JUSTICE GORSUCH
turns.text: Like oil states, this case involves the Patent Office's new Inter Partes Review procedure. Inter Partes Review is an administrative proceeding that allows private parties to challenge previously issued patents in an adversarial process before the Patent Office. Here the question we face is not a constitutional one, but a statutory one. When the Patent Office institutes an Inter Partes Review of a patent, must it decide the patentability of every claim the petitioner has challenged or may it resolve only some of those claims? The question arose in this case after petitioner SAS Institute sought an Inter Partes Review of respondent ComplementSoft's software patent. In its petition SAS challenged all 16 of the patents claims, but the Director of the Patent Office, relying on a regulation that provides for something called partial institution, claimed the discretionary power to institute review on only some of those claims and deny review on the rest. The case then proceeded before the Patent Office's Patent Trial and Appeal Board, which eventually issued a final written decision addressing the claims on which the Director had chosen to institute review. But that decision did not address the remaining claims on which the Director had refused review. This led SAS to seek review in the Federal Circuit, where it argued that the Board must decide the patentability of every patent claim a petitioner has challenged, not just some. The Federal Circuit rejected this argument and re-granted certiorari to decide the question for ourselves. We find the statutory text supplies a clear answer. Section 318(a) of the Inter Partes Review statute says that if an Inter Partes Review is instituted and not dismissed, the Board shall issue a final written decision with respect to the patentability of any patent claim challenged by the petitioner. This directive is both mandatory and comprehensive. The word shall imposes a nondiscretionary duty and the word any bears an expansive meaning that in context refers to every member of the particular class or group. Accordingly, the statute means when Inter Partes Review is instituted the Board must decide the patentability of every claim the petitioner has challenged. The Director enjoys no discretion to curate the claims at issue and where a statute's language carries a plain meaning, the duty of an administrative agency is to follow the commands as written, not replace them with those of its own choosing. As we explained in today's opinion, the larger statutory structure confirms that the Inter Partes Review proceedings are to be shaped from start to finish by the petitioner's petition, not by the Director's discretion. Under the statute's terms the process starts with a petition that identifies each patent claimed and the grounds for the challenge. The Director must then decide whether to institute an Inter Partes Review pursuant to the petition. If he institutes review, the patent owner must file a response to the petition and at the end of the litigation the Board must issue a decision that addresses every claim challenged by the petitioner. Under these express terms there is no room for partial institution or practice that allows a Director to depart from the petition and use his discretion to define the scope of the proceedings. The Director offers several replies, but we find none persuasive. Here are just two examples. The Director says that even if we disagree with his reading of the statute, we should still defer to it under a case called Chevron v. Natural Resources Defense Council. SAS for its part suggests that we should abandon Chevron and hold that the meaning of the statutory term is properly for a judicial, not administrative judgment. For now, we can leave that question for another day, whether Chevron should remain. Even under Chevron, courts owe no deference to an agency's interpretation of the law unless after employing traditional tools of statutory construction we cannot discern Congress' meaning. Here for all the reasons given we are left with no uncertainty about what the statute means. Alternatively, the Director suggests we lack the power even to consider the legality of his partial institution practice. He points to Section 314(d), which says the Director's determination whether to institute review shall be final and non-appealable. But as we held two years ago in a case called Cuozzo Speed Technologies v. Lee, this provision precludes judicial review only of the Director's initial determination that there is a reasonable likelihood the petitioner's challenge would succeed. Cuozzo explained that if a party believes the Patent Office has exceeded its statutory authority, judicial review remains available, and that of course is exactly the kind of argument SAS has raised here. For these reasons and others given at today's opinion, we reverse the judgment of the Federal Circuit and remand for further proceedings consistent with our opinion. Justice Ginsburg has filed a dissenting opinion, in which Justices Breyer, Sotomayor and Kagan join. Justice Breyer has also filed a dissenting opinion, in which Justices Ginsburg and Sotomayor join and in which Justice Kagan joins except as to Part 3(a).
